URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA

SUMMARY:
A **non-coding RNA** (**ncRNA**) is a functional RNA molecule that is not translated "Translation (genetics)") into a protein. The DNA sequence from which a functional non-coding RNA is transcribed is often called an RNA gene. Internal ribosome entry sites (IRES) are RNA structures that allow for translation "Translation (genetics)") initiation in the middle of a mRNA sequence as part of the process of protein synthesis._[citation needed_] | Ribonucleic acids (coding, non-coding) | | Translational | * Messenger * precursor, heterogenous nuclear * modified Messenger * Transfer * Ribosomal * Transfer-messenger | | --- | | Regulatory | * Interferential * Micro * Small interfering * Piwi-interacting * Antisense * Processual * Small nuclear * Small nucleolar * Small Cajal Body RNAs * Y RNA * Enhancer RNAs | | Others | * Guide * Ribozyme * Small hairpin * Small temporal * Trans-acting small interfering * Subgenomic messenger | |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Compare [untranslated regions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Untranslated_region "Untranslated region").

Class of ribonucleic acid that is not translated into proteins

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/NcRNAs-central-dogma.svg/500px-NcRNAs-central-dogma.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:NcRNAs-central-dogma.svg)

The roles of non-coding RNAs: [Ribonucleoproteins](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribonucleoprotein "Ribonucleoprotein") are shown in red, non-coding RNAs in blue.

A **non-coding RNA** (**ncRNA**) is a functional [RNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNA "RNA") molecule that is not [translated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)") into a [protein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein "Protein"). The [DNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DNA "DNA") sequence from which a functional non-coding RNA is transcribed is often called an RNA [gene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gene "Gene"). Abundant and functionally important [types of non-coding RNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_RNAs "List of RNAs") include [transfer RNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transfer_RNA "Transfer RNA") (tRNAs) and [ribosomal RNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribosomal_RNA "Ribosomal RNA") (rRNAs), as well as small RNAs such as [microRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MicroRNA "MicroRNA"), [siRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SiRNA "SiRNA"), [piRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/PiRNA "PiRNA"), [snoRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SnoRNA "SnoRNA"), [snRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SnRNA "SnRNA"), [exRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extracellular_RNA "Extracellular RNA"), [scaRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ScaRNAs "ScaRNAs") and the [long ncRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Long_noncoding_RNA "Long noncoding RNA") such as [Xist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xist "Xist") and [HOTAIR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HOTAIR "HOTAIR").

The number of non-coding RNAs within the human genome is unknown; however, recent [transcriptomic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transcriptomics "Transcriptomics") and [bioinformatic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioinformatics "Bioinformatics") studies suggest that there are thousands of non-coding transcripts.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid15790807-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid17571346-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Thind-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid17568003-4)[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-MorrisKV-5)[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Shahrouki_P_2012-6)[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-7) Many of the newly identified ncRNAs have unknown functions, if any.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid15851066-8) There is no consensus on how much of non-coding transcription is functional: some believe most ncRNAs to be non-functional "junk RNA", spurious transcriptions,[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-waste-9)[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-PalazzoLee2015-10) while others expect that many non-coding transcripts have functions to be discovered.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-12)

History and discovery
---------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Non-coding_RNA&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and discovery")]

Further information: [History of molecular biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_molecular_biology "History of molecular biology")

[Nucleic acids](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleic_acid "Nucleic acid") were first discovered in 1868 by [Friedrich Miescher](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Friedrich_Miescher "Friedrich Miescher"),[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-13) and by 1939, RNA had been implicated in [protein synthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Protein_biosynthesis "Protein biosynthesis").[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-14) Two decades later, [Francis Crick](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Francis_Crick "Francis Crick") predicted a functional RNA component which mediated [translation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)"); he reasoned that RNA is better suited to base-pair with an mRNA transcript than a pure [polypeptide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polypeptide "Polypeptide").[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-15)

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/TRNA-Phe_yeast_1ehz.png/250px-TRNA-Phe_yeast_1ehz.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:TRNA-Phe_yeast_1ehz.png)

The cloverleaf structure of Yeast tRNA Phe (_inset_) and the 3D structure determined by X-ray analysis.

The first non-coding RNA to be characterised was an [alanine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alanine "Alanine") tRNA found in [baker's yeast](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Baker%27s_yeast "Baker's yeast"), its structure was published in 1965.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Hol65-16) To produce a purified alanine tRNA sample, [Robert W. Holley](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_W._Holley "Robert W. Holley")_et al._ used 140[kg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram") of commercial baker's yeast to give just 1[g](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gram "Gram") of purified tRNA Ala for analysis.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Nobel68-17) The 80 [nucleotide](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleotide "Nucleotide") tRNA was sequenced by first being digested with [Pancreatic ribonuclease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pancreatic_ribonuclease "Pancreatic ribonuclease") (producing fragments ending in [Cytosine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cytosine "Cytosine") or [Uridine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uridine "Uridine")) and then with takadiastase ribonuclease Tl (producing fragments which finished with [Guanosine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guanosine "Guanosine")). [Chromatography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromatography "Chromatography") and identification of the 5' and 3' ends then helped arrange the fragments to establish the RNA sequence.[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Nobel68-17) Of the three structures originally proposed for this tRNA,[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Hol65-16) the 'cloverleaf' structure was independently proposed in several following publications.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid5938777-18)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-19)[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-20)[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-21) The cloverleaf [secondary structure](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secondary_structure "Secondary structure") was finalised following [X-ray crystallography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") analysis performed by two independent research groups in 1974.[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-22)[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-23)

[Ribosomal RNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribosomal_RNA "Ribosomal RNA") was next to be discovered, followed by URNA in the early 1980s. Since then, the discovery of new non-coding RNAs has continued with [snoRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SnoRNAs "SnoRNAs"), [Xist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xist "Xist"), [CRISPR](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/CRISPR "CRISPR") and many more.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Edd01-24) Recent notable additions include [riboswitches](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riboswitch "Riboswitch") and [miRNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MiRNA "MiRNA"); the discovery of the RNAi [mechanism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mechanism_(biology) "Mechanism (biology)") associated with the latter earned [Craig C. Mello](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Craig_C._Mello "Craig C. Mello") and [Andrew Fire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Andrew_Fire "Andrew Fire") the 2006 [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine").[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-25)

Recent discoveries of ncRNAs have been achieved through both experimental and [bioinformatic methods](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioinformatics_discovery_of_non-coding_RNAs "Bioinformatics discovery of non-coding RNAs").

Biological roles
----------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Non-coding_RNA&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Biological roles")]

Noncoding RNAs belong to several groups and are involved in many cellular processes.[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Monga-26) These range from ncRNAs of central importance that are conserved across all or most cellular life through to more transient ncRNAs specific to one or a few closely related species. The more conserved ncRNAs are thought to be [molecular fossils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_fossil "Molecular fossil") or relics from the [last universal common ancestor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LUCA "LUCA") and the [RNA world](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNA_world_hypothesis "RNA world hypothesis"), and their current roles remain mostly in regulation of information flow from DNA to protein.[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid9419222-27)[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid9419221-28)[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-pmid10497339-29)

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/010_large_subunit-1FFK.gif/250px-010_large_subunit-1FFK.gif)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:010_large_subunit-1FFK.gif)

Atomic structure of the 50S Subunit from [_Haloarcula marismortui_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haloarcula "Haloarcula"). Proteins are shown in blue and the two RNA strands in orange and yellow.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Ban-30) The small patch of green in the center of the subunit is the active site.

### In translation

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Non-coding_RNA&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In translation")]

Many of the conserved, essential and abundant ncRNAs are involved in [translation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Translation_(genetics) "Translation (genetics)"). [Ribonucleoprotein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribonucleoprotein "Ribonucleoprotein") (RNP) particles called [ribosomes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribosome "Ribosome") are the 'factories' where translation takes place in the cell. The ribosome consists of more than 60% [ribosomal RNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RRNA "RRNA"); these are made up of 3 ncRNAs in [prokaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prokaryotes "Prokaryotes") and 4 ncRNAs in [eukaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryotes "Eukaryotes"). Ribosomal RNAs catalyse the translation of nucleotide sequences to protein. Another set of ncRNAs, [Transfer RNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transfer_RNA "Transfer RNA"), form an 'adaptor molecule' between [mRNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MRNA "MRNA") and protein. The [H/ACA box and C/D box snoRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SnoRNA "SnoRNA") are ncRNAs found in archaea and eukaryotes. [RNase MRP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNase_MRP "RNase MRP") is restricted to eukaryotes. Both groups of ncRNA are involved in the maturation of rRNA. The snoRNAs guide covalent modifications of rRNA, tRNA and [snRNAs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SnRNA "SnRNA"); RNase MRP cleaves the [internal transcribed spacer 1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internal_transcribed_spacer_1 "Internal transcribed spacer 1") between 18S and 5.8S rRNAs. The ubiquitous ncRNA, [RNase P](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNase_P "RNase P"), is an evolutionary relative of RNase MRP.[[31]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-PMID16540690-31) RNase P matures tRNA sequences by generating mature 5'-ends of tRNAs through cleaving the 5'-leader elements of precursor-tRNAs. Another ubiquitous RNP called [SRP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Signal_recognition_particle "Signal recognition particle") recognizes and transports specific nascent proteins to the [endoplasmic reticulum](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Endoplasmic_reticulum "Endoplasmic reticulum") in [eukaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") and the [plasma membrane](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plasma_membrane "Plasma membrane") in [prokaryotes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prokaryote "Prokaryote"). In bacteria, [Transfer-messenger RNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TmRNA "TmRNA") (tmRNA) is an RNP involved in rescuing stalled ribosomes, tagging incomplete [polypeptides](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peptide "Peptide") and promoting the degradation of aberrant mRNA.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

### In RNA splicing

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Non-coding_RNA&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In RNA splicing")]

[![Image 7](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Yeast_tri-snRNP.jpg/250px-Yeast_tri-snRNP.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Yeast_tri-snRNP.jpg)

Electron microscopy images of the yeast spliceosome. Note the bulk of the complex is in fact ncRNA.

In eukaryotes, the [spliceosome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spliceosome "Spliceosome") performs the [splicing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/RNA_splicing "RNA splicing") reactions essential for removing [intron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intron "Intron") sequences, this process is required for the formation of mature [mRNA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MRNA "MRNA"). The [spliceosome](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spliceosome "Spliceosome") is another RNP often known as the [snRNP](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SnRNP "SnRNP") or tri-snRNP. There are two different forms of the spliceosome, the major and minor forms. The ncRNA components of the major spliceosome are [U1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U1_snRNA "U1 snRNA"), [U2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U2_snRNA "U2 snRNA"), [U4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U4_snRNA "U4 snRNA"), [U5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U5_snRNA "U5 snRNA"), and [U6](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U6_snRNA "U6 snRNA"). The ncRNA components of the minor spliceosome are [U11](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U11_snRNA "U11 snRNA"), [U12](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U12_snRNA "U12 snRNA"), [U5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U5_snRNA "U5 snRNA"), [U4atac](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U4atac_minor_spliceosomal_RNA "U4atac minor spliceosomal RNA") and [U6atac](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/U6atac_minor_spliceosomal_RNA "U6atac minor spliceosomal RNA").[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

Another group of introns can catalyse their own removal from host transcripts; these are called self-splicing RNAs. There are two main groups of self-splicing RNAs: [group I catalytic intron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_I_catalytic_intron "Group I catalytic intron") and [group II catalytic intron](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Group_II_catalytic_intron "Group II catalytic intron"). These ncRNAs catalyze their own excision from mRNA, tRNA and rRNA precursors in a wide range of organisms.[_[citation needed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation\_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")_]

In mammals it has been found that snoRNAs can also regulate the [alternative splicing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alternative_splicing "Alternative splicing") of mRNA, for example snoRNA [HBII-52](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Small_nucleolar_RNA_SNORD115 "Small nucleolar RNA SNORD115") regulates the splicing of [serotonin receptor 2C](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/5-HT2C_receptor "5-HT2C receptor").[[32]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-Kishore-32)

In nematodes, the [SmY](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SmY "SmY") ncRNA appears to be involved in mRNA [trans-splicing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trans-splicing "Trans-splicing").[[33]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-coding_RNA#cite_note-33)

## Abstract

Evidence accumulated over the past decade shows that long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) are widely expressed and have key roles in gene regulation. Recent studies have begun to unravel how the biogenesis of lncRNAs is distinct from that of mRNAs and is linked with their specific subcellular localizations and functions. Depending on their localization and their specific interactions with DNA, RNA and proteins, lncRNAs can modulate chromatin function, regulate the assembly and function of membraneless nuclear bodies, alter the stability and translation of cytoplasmic mRNAs and interfere with signalling pathways. Many of these functions ultimately affect gene expression in diverse biological and physiopathological contexts, such as in neuronal disorders, immune responses and cancer. Tissue-specific and condition-specific expression patterns suggest that lncRNAs are potential biomarkers and provide a rationale to target them clinically. In this Review, we discuss the mechanisms of lncRNA biogenesis, localization and functions in transcriptional, post-transcriptional and other modes of gene regulation, and their potential therapeutic applications.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs) include diverse types like microRNAs (miRNAs), small interfering RNAs (siRNAs), long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs), and ribosomal RNAs (rRNAs), each serving distinct regulatory roles.

ncRNAs regulate gene expression through various mechanisms, including transcriptional interference, RNA splicing regulation, and post-transcriptional modifications, influencing protein synthesis and cellular functions.

ncRNAs play crucial roles in gene regulation by modulating transcription and translation processes, impacting cellular pathways, and maintaining homeostasis in physiological and pathological conditions.
Many ncRNAs interact with chromatin, influencing chromatin structure and dynamics, thus regulating gene accessibility and expression, which is critical for cellular identity and function.

Due to their regulatory roles, ncRNAs are emerging as potential therapeutic targets for various diseases, including cancer and genetic disorders, offering novel strategies for treatment interventions.
